evals.expected_output: Refactored Python code using guard clauses, extracted helper, named constants, plus explicit mention of what was deliberately NOT changed and why.
assertions: Guard clauses (early returns) are used to exit for None/wrong-status at the top, eliminating the 4-level deep nesting
The duplicated tax+fee calculation block (present 3 times in original) is extracted into a single named helper function
Magic numbers are replaced with named constants: at minimum 0.08 (tax rate), 10000 (high-value threshold), and 25 (high-value fee)
Output includes a '## Changes' block with 'What changed:', 'Why:', and 'Deliberately NOT changed:' sections
The 'Deliberately NOT changed' section explicitly mentions at least one thing that was intentionally preserved (e.g. the print side-effect, in-place mutation) with a reason why it was left alone
Behavior is preserved: the refactored code sets final_total, sets processed=True, and returns the order dict with the same logic

evals.expected_output: Stops, names the bug, preserves buggy line in refactored code, asks user for confirmation before fixing.
assertions: The output explicitly identifies the pre-existing bug: prevPage uses 'page > 0' but should use 'page > 1', causing prevPage=0 instead of null when page=1
The buggy condition 'page > 0' (not 'page > 1') remains present verbatim in any refactored code shown, and the output explicitly asks the user whether to fix it as a separate step
Structural improvements are still delivered (e.g., const instead of var, object literal return) even while the bug is left in place
Output includes a '## Changes' block with 'What changed:', 'Why:', and 'Deliberately NOT changed:' sections
The 'Deliberately NOT changed' section mentions the pre-existing bug and explains why it was not fixed in this refactor

evals.expected_output: Asks for confirmation before the API change. Does not include a complete production-ready refactored implementation without confirmation.
assertions: The output asks for user confirmation before proceeding AND does not include a complete production-ready refactored implementation — it may show a preview or phase plan but the actual signature change requires explicit user approval
The output identifies the specific blast radius: changing the function signature would require updating 15+ call sites
The output mentions that no test coverage exists as a risk factor for applying this structural change
If the output eventually provides refactored code, it includes a '## Changes' block with 'What changed:', 'Why:', and 'Deliberately NOT changed:' sections

evals.expected_output: Asks what the user finds hardest before touching anything. Produces no refactored code in the same response.
assertions: The output asks a clarifying question about what the user finds hardest before making any changes — not a generic question, but one specifically scoped to understanding the user's pain with this code
The output produces no refactored code at all — not as a main response, not as a preview, not as 'here's what I'd do while I wait for your answer'
Once refactoring proceeds, output includes a '## Changes' block with 'What changed:', 'Why:', and 'Deliberately NOT changed:' sections

evals.expected_output: Suggests ruff/black before any manual formatting. Any reformatted code is attributed to the tool.
assertions: The output recommends running an automated formatting tool (ruff, black, or equivalent) as the first or primary step, before presenting any manually-edited code
If the output shows reformatted code, it is explicitly attributed to the automated tool (e.g. 'after running ruff format') — not presented as manual edits the agent made line by line
If additional refactoring follows the formatting step, output includes a '## Changes' block with 'What changed:', 'Why:', and 'Deliberately NOT changed:' sections

evals.expected_output: Reasons both sides — argues the case AGAINST merging (divergence risk) before deciding, not just the case for it. States a condition that makes extraction safe.
assertions: The output explicitly argues the case AGAINST extraction — giving at least one concrete scenario in which shipping and billing validation would need to diverge (e.g. VAT rules, international formats, different compliance requirements)
The output does not treat extraction as obviously correct — it acknowledges the business-concept distinction between shipping and billing addresses, even if it ultimately decides to extract

If the output proposes extraction, it names a specific condition that makes it safe (e.g. 'only if both address types will always share the same field validation rules') rather than declaring extraction unconditionally correct
If extraction is applied, output includes a '## Changes' block with 'What changed:', 'Why:', and 'Deliberately NOT changed:' sections, noting the condition under which extraction is safe
